Additionally, SLVP tracks more than just silver, as it includes other metals as well, while SLVR may be one of the best 'pure play' ETFs if one has a bullish silver thesis, as it's exclusively tied to the metal.

SLVR's expense ratio is on the higher side relative to the median for most ETFs at 0.65%; however, this is still in line with its peers such as SILJ and SIL, meaning I still believe it is competitively priced when considering its alternatives.

Risks

Although silver prices may have found a stronger price floor due to its structural supply and demand deficits, investors must still be cognizant that the silver market is tiny relative to other global assets or even compared to gold, at one-tenth of its market size. This means that its rallies and sell-offs can come suddenly and be seemingly disconnected from the market fundamentals. A narrow set of traders relative to other industries can change the trajectory of the silver spot price.

Silver has also historically exhibited strong mean-reversion tendencies with sell-offs that flatten its value over the long run to be more in line with its moving average. Therefore, despite the strong rallies and my own optimistic forecasts, I think silver is uniquely risky due to its liquidity pool but not necessarily more so than traditional growth assets. I would not be comfortable having a large allocation in silver at this stage.

Conclusion

Overall, despite the risks, I rate SLVR a buy. The continued supply constraints as well as demand tailwinds are attractive, and with appropriate position sizing, investors can reach an attractive risk-return trade-off. I then reiterate my rating for the ETF and expect good things to come for investors who hold it in the future.

data.title: T. Rowe Price: Structural Issues Aren't Easy To Fix
data.description:
As I covered about two years ago, T. Rowe Price Group (TROW) was suffering from relatively weak investment performance that resulted in net outflows, a trend that was not easy to reverse as the company is largely exposed to active funds, which face structural headwinds as investors are increasingly moving money to passive funds. Despite that, its shares were trading at a premium to peers at the time, which didn’t make much sense in my opinion. Not surprisingly, the performance of its shares has been quite weak over the past couple of years, underperforming the overall stock market by a wide margin.

This weak performance is largely justified by the same trend that TROW was experiencing a couple of years ago, as most issues were structural rather than cyclical. Indeed, as can be seen in the next graph, most of its funds are not outperforming passive alternatives and benchmarks for several historical time periods, with performance being especially negative when considering the 1-year and 3-year periods.

As an active investment management company, the most important distinctive factor of TROW to its competitors is investment performance, as usually investors look for Funds that have historically beat their competitors and respective benchmarks. Given that TROW has a poor track record in recent years across Equities, Fixed Income, and Multi-Asset, it’s not surprising that it has reported net outflows over the past few years.

Indeed, while 2022 was a negative year for capital markets in general, and that could explain net outflows in that particular year, TROW has struggled to be competitive in the asset management industry and continued to report net outflows over the past three years, during a positive period for capital markets.

On the other hand, despite net outflows since 2021 TROW’s assets under management (AuM) were boosted by positive market changes in recent years, reaching a new record high close to $1.8 trillion at the end of last September.

However, despite record high AuMs, the trend of persisting net outflows over the past five years is quite concerning for business growth and raises questions about TROW’s competitive position in the industry. Moreover, considering its weak investment performance in recent years, this is a trend that is not expected to reverse soon. For instance, only 27% of Equity Funds are beating passive alternatives over the last year, and only 19% are above benchmarks, showing a very poor performance that is clearly an important headwind for flows ahead.

Given this backdrop, it’s not surprising to see that TROW had about $8 billion in net outflows during the last quarter (Q3 2025), which was nonetheless an improvement compared to net outflows of nearly $15 billion in the previous quarter. During the first nine months of 2025, its net outflows amounted to more than $31 billion, making it quite likely that net outflows for the full year will be similar to the levels reported last year (net outflows of $43 billion in 2024).

Investors should note that net outflows/inflows are critical in asset management because they represent ‘organic’ growth, or lack of it, while capital market changes are the same for all companies in the industry. Flows are mostly a good indicator to know the level of investor confidence in the company’s product offering, its competitive strength in the industry, and future growth potential. Moreover, asset managers earn revenue based on AuMs and net inflows are an important driver of increasing fee income, while on the other hand, sustained net outflows shrink revenues and earnings over the long term.

Considering that TROW has been reporting net outflows for five consecutive years, during periods of positive and negative returns in the capital markets, this is an important headwind for business growth over the next few years. Given that investment performance has not improved lately, it’s quite likely that TROW will continue to experience net outflows across its three most important asset classes, but more specifically in U.S. large-cap equity Funds that have shown poor performance in the recent past, putting into question its ability to reach its 1-3% organic-growth target in the medium term.

The street also seems to be quite negative about the company’s ability to reverse net outflows over the next two years, given that current consensus is for TROW to have net outflows of about $37 billion in 2026 and around $22 billion in 2027. While there is a high degree of uncertainty about net inflows/outflows ahead, I also think it’s more likely for net outflows to continue as investment performance remains weak and an inflection doesn’t seem likely in the short term.

This expectation is also supported by the company’s AuM mix, which is highly exposed to the domestic market, plus Equities is the largest asset class with close to $900 billion of AuM at the end of last September. Given that TROW is showing poor investment returns in Equities and the U.S. market is the one most affected by the gradually move of investing money away from active to passive funds, this does not bode well for net flows ahead.

From a business strategy perspective, TROW’s profile has not changed much over the past few years, and the company has not made significant efforts, in my opinion, to further diversify its business, even though it has pushed for growth in multi-asset and alternative funds in recent years. However, while competitors like BlackRock (BLK) or Invesco (IVZ) moved their business toward a mix of active and passive funds, TROW maintained its strategy of differentiation through active management, seeking to deliver alpha through internal capabilities of sound portfolio management and in-house research. However, like many of its competitors in the active asset management industry, its track record has not been great in recent years, putting into question the sustainability of its business model over the long term.

Moreover, on top of net outflows that are a structural headwind for its business, the company has also been facing fee compression as competition in the industry is fierce and investors increasingly demand lower fees from active funds, as the differentiation from passive funds has not been great in the recent past. Not surprisingly, TROW’s effective fee rate (EFR) has been declining like it has happened across the industry, from an average level of about 90 basis points (bps) in 2000 (for the U.S. mutual fund industry) to only about 35 bps in 2025. TROW’s effective fee rate was 39 bps in Q3 2025 (without performance fees), which is above average, but declined by about 180 bps during the last year.

This fee compression is clearly negative for the company’s top-line and earnings growth and has a greater impact on its financial performance than net outflows, as rising capital markets were more than enough to boost management fees in recent years.

Given this background, TROW’s revenues amounted to nearly $1.9 billion in Q3 2025, representing annual growth of about 6%. This is justified by an increase of 8.2% on average AuMs in Q3 2025 compared to the same quarter of last year, but its revenue growth rate was lower than AuM growth, showing that TROW’s fee compression is a significant headwind for revenue growth.

Another issue regarding the company’s operating leverage is rising expenses, as the inflationary environment has put pressure on wages and overall administrative expenses. In Q3 2025, TROW’s operating expenses amounted to $1.25 billion, up by 6.7% YoY. Its cost-to-income ratio was 66% in the quarter, which is not fantastic in the asset management industry, and showed a deterioration of more than 30 bps compared to the same quarter of last year.

Its operating income was $643 million in the quarter (+4.9% YoY) and its operating margin declined by 40 bps to 34%, still being a good level of profitability in the industry considering that the average margin is between 30% and 32%.

Going forward, it’s quite likely that TROW will report net outflows in Q4 given that the company reported total AuMs of $1.79 trillion in October (slightly up compared to September) and net outflows of $5.9 billion in the month. The company has recently announced a strategic partnership with Goldman Sachs (GS) aiming to deliver public and private market solutions to customers, with some product launches expected during 2026. This may be an important contribution to an inflection on flows over the coming quarters, but so far it is quite uncertain what the impact of this new partnership will be on the company’s net flows during the next year, and this partnership doesn’t seem to be enough to change its flow trend over the next few quarters.

Regarding its balance sheet, TROW maintains a very strong position with a net cash position of about $4.3 billion, allowing it to return significant capital to shareholders. During the last quarter, TROW returned $442 million through dividends and share buybacks, representing about 70% of its operating income in the period. As the company’s financial position is sound and TROW does not need to retain many earnings ahead, I think its capital return strategy is not likely to change much in the near future, being a combination of a growing dividend and share buybacks.

Its current quarterly dividend is $1.27 per share, which at its current share price leads to a dividend yield of about 4.75%, but looking at current dividend estimates, the street is expecting small dividend growth ahead. While this looks conservative, as TROW’s earnings are increasing at mid-single digits and the company has a sound financial position, I think TROW is likely to raise its dividend like it did last year, probably increasing its quarterly dividend by 2-3% to a level around $1.30 per share when it announces its 2025 earnings at the beginning of next year (probably in February).

Regarding its valuation, TROW is currently trading at around 11x earnings, a much lower valuation compared to when I last analyzed it (around 16x earnings at the time). While TROW was trading at a premium valuation to peers a couple of years ago, the market has de-rated its shares as it became aware that its issues were structural rather than cyclical and that TROW was not likely to reverse its poor investment performance soon, justifying why it’s now trading at a discount to peers (that trade on average at more than 12x earnings). I see this discount as warranted considering that TROW continues to face many issues that aren’t easy to fix, and I’m not expecting an improved operating performance over the next couple of years.

Conclusion

T. Rowe Price doesn’t seem to hold any competitive advantage in the asset management industry, as the company’s strategy to focus on active funds has clearly backfired in recent years, as investment performance is not great across its Funds. This has resulted in net outflows during the past five years, which, together with margin compression and higher expenses, have led to muted earnings growth that was only offset by rising capital markets. Given this backdrop, its shares have de-rated to a lower earnings multiple than in the past and offer a relatively high dividend yield, being the main reasons why I maintain my ‘hold’ rating despite a relatively muted business outlook.
